perturbation.original_text: 1.1 “Confidential Information ” refers to: 1.1.1 In respect of Information provided in documentary form or in other electronic form, Information which at the time of provision is marked or otherwise designated to show expressly or by necessary implication that is imparted in confidence; In respect of Information that is imparted orally, any Information that the Discloser or its re presentatives informed the Recipient at the time of disclosure was imparted in confidence; 1.1.2
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 “Confidential Information ” refers to: 1.1.1 In respect of Information provided in documentary form or in other electronic form, Information which at the time of provision is marked or otherwise designated to show expressly or by necessary implication that is imparted in confidence; In respect of Information that is imparted orally, any Information that the Discloser or its representatives believed was imparted in confidence; 1.1.2
perturbation.explanation: Changing "informed" to "believed" introduces subjectivity and removes the requirement of clear communication, potentially conflicting with Singapore's laws on contractual certainty and reasonable understanding between parties. The original definition requires explicit notification of confidentiality, while the modified version only requires the discloser to 'believe' it was communicated, weakening the objective standard.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Misrepresentation and contractual certainty
perturbation.law_explanation: Singapore contract law requires clear communication and understanding between parties. Changing 'informed' to 'believed' weakens the objective standard required for establishing a meeting of minds, potentially making the confidentiality clause unenforceable due to uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'informed' to 'believed' introduces a significant contradiction by weakening the requirement for clear communication, which is essential for contract enforceability. The original text necessitates explicit notification of confidentiality, providing an objective standard. The modified version, however, only requires the discloser to 'believe' that confidentiality was communicated, creating a subjective standard. The scraped snippet highlights the importance of mutual assent in contracts, stating that 'The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent, expressed by a valid offer and acceptance'. Changing 'informed' to 'believed' undermines the objective element of mutual assent, as it relies on a party's subjective belief rather than a verifiable communication. This shift creates uncertainty and could render the confidentiality clause unenforceable, as it becomes challenging to prove that both parties clearly understood the terms of confidentiality at the time of disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1.1 Receive and keep the Confidential Information secret and confidential and not disclose such Confidential Information to any third party
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1.1 Receive and keep the Confidential Information secret and confidential and not disclose such Confidential Information to any third party, unless such disclosure is deemed necessary for business efficacy.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless such disclosure is deemed necessary for business efficacy' introduces a loophole that contradicts the NDA's purpose. This 'business efficacy' exception lacks a clear definition, potentially allowing the Recipient to disclose information based on their own interpretation, violating the intended strict confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contract Law - Implied Terms and Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: While Singapore law allows for implied terms, this addition is too broad and subjective. It undermines the express terms of the NDA (strict confidentiality) and introduces uncertainty, potentially violating the principle of good faith in contract performance. The lack of a clear definition for 'business efficacy' is problematic.

perturbation.justification: The added clause "unless such disclosure is deemed necessary for business efficacy" creates a loophole that undermines the original strict confidentiality obligation. The term 'business efficacy' is not clearly defined, allowing the recipient to subjectively determine when disclosure is permissible. This contradicts the purpose of an NDA, which is to maintain confidentiality. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address NDAs or business efficacy, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight the problem of introducing a subjective and undefined exception that weakens the original contractual obligation, which aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' as contractual language that deviates from legally recognized definitions.

perturbation.original_text: 5.  Intellectual Property in the Confidential Information The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the intellectual property and in particular, the copyright in the Confidential Information disclosed by the Discloser, including any documents, files and other items containing any Confidential Information belongs to the Discloser. 5.2 This Agreement is neither to prejudice nor limit the rights of the Discloser in respect of any intellectual property rights in the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.  Intellectual Property in the Confidential Information The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the intellectual property and in particular, the copyright in the Confidential Information disclosed by the Discloser, including any documents, files and other items containing any Confidential Information belongs to the Discloser. 5.2 This Agreement is to allow the rights of the Discloser in respect of any intellectual property rights in the Confidential Information to be jointly held by both parties..
perturbation.explanation: Changing "neither to prejudice nor limit the rights" to "to allow the rights... to be jointly held" fundamentally alters the ownership of the intellectual property. Original agreement clearly states all IP belongs to Discloser. This change implies a joint ownership, which requires a explicit assignment/agreement, which is not outlined, contradicting Singapore's Copyright Act.
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore Copyright Act - Ownership of Copyright
perturbation.law_explanation: The Singapore Copyright Act stipulates the conditions for copyright ownership and transfer. This modification attempts to create joint ownership without fulfilling the necessary legal requirements (e.g., written assignment), potentially leading to disputes over IP rights and conflicting with the Act's provisions.

perturbation.justification: The original text clearly assigns all intellectual property rights to the Discloser. The changed text alters this fundamental assignment by stating the rights will be 'jointly held by both parties.' This directly contradicts the original agreement's explicit statement of ownership. Although the law snippet is not directly applicable, the perturbation explanation indicates this change requires a explicit assignment/agreement, which is not outlined, potentially conflicting with copyright law principles of ownership.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 Third Parties This Agreement does not create any right enforceable by any person who is not a party to it under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev. Ed).
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 Third Parties This Agreement does create rights enforceable by any person who is a beneficiary of the contract, even if they are not a direct party to it, superseding the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev. Ed).
perturbation.explanation: Changing "does not create any right enforceable" to "does create rights enforceable" directly contradicts the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act. The Act allows certain third parties to enforce contractual terms if the contract expressly provides for it or if the term purports to confer a benefit on them. By stating rights are created for any 'beneficiary,' regardless of the Act's conditions, this clause creates legal uncertainty and contradicts the Act's specific provisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act
perturbation.law_citation: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev. Ed)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act in Singapore allows third parties to enforce contractual terms only under specific conditions (express provision or conferring a benefit). This modification attempts to grant enforcement rights to any 'beneficiary,' regardless of whether those conditions are met, directly contradicting the Act and potentially invalidating the clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: third party beneficiary | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you third party beneficiary A third party beneficiary is a person benefiting from a contract made between two other parties, where the two contracting parties intended to benefit the third party beneficiary. The third party beneficiary is not a party to the contract, but has rights under the contract since it was made with an intent to benefit them. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract law Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act. The original text explicitly excludes rights for non-parties, aligning with the Act's intent. The changed text grants rights to any 'beneficiary,' regardless of the Act's specific conditions (express provision or conferring a benefit), thus broadening the scope beyond what the Act allows. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports the existence of third-party beneficiaries, but highlights the need for 'intent to benefit the third party beneficiary' from the contracting parties. The modified text bypasses this 'intent' requirement, creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation confirm this contradiction by noting that the modified clause attempts to grant enforcement rights beyond the Act's stipulated conditions.
